text.title: QA Risk Matrix Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every risk matrix must satisfy. These prevent the two dominant failure modes: all-areas-high (matrix has no signal) and stale-matrix (matrix reflects last year's risk landscape).

rule.statement: The matrix lists product areas at a consistent granularity — neither "Backend" (too coarse, no testing strategy applies) nor "user.controller.create method" (too fine, drowns the signal). Target 8-20 areas. Areas are enumerated explicitly in risk-matrix.yaml; new top-level features add a new area.

rationale.source: risk-decomposition research; sample QA risk matrices from teams of various sizes

rule.rationale: Without an explicit enumeration, the matrix becomes an unbounded list that nobody can read. The 8-20 range is the empirical sweet spot for a single team's working memory and for meaningful investment allocation.

example.title: Risk-matrix area list
example.code: areas: - auth-login-and-signup - billing-subscription-lifecycle - billing-invoice-rendering - search-and-filtering - email-onboarding-flow - notifications-in-app - data-export-csv-and-api - admin-impersonation-and-audit-log - public-api-rate-limiting - tenant-isolation-and-data-residency

rule.statement: Impact score (1-5) is anchored to a customer-or-business-effect rubric: 5 = revenue loss in single-digit hours, data loss, or regulatory breach; 4 = visible customer trust loss; 3 = degraded functionality with workaround; 2 = annoyance; 1 = invisible. Impact MUST be backed by a specific worst-case sentence, not a feeling.

rationale.source: business-impact analysis frameworks; ISTQB risk-based testing rubric

rule.rationale: Impact-from-feeling produces flat 4s and 5s across the board. A customer-effect anchor forces honesty: "billing-invoice-rendering breaking costs $X/hour in dispute support; impact 4". Different areas now have different scores, which is the entire point.

rule.statement: Likelihood score (1-5) is computed from data, not opinion: incident frequency over the past 6 months, commit churn over the same window, novelty (new code in the last 30 days), and complexity proxies (cyclomatic, dependency-graph depth). `scripts/likelihood-from-incidents.py` produces the raw score; the engineer can adjust ±1 with a documented reason.

rationale.source: risk-based testing research; common bias in subjective likelihood ratings

rule.rationale: Subjective likelihood ratings cluster around 3. Data-anchored scores spread out and identify the areas that have actually broken or churned recently. Engineers retain final judgment via the ±1 adjustment, but the prior comes from the data.

rule.statement: The matrix divides into 4 quadrants by impact (high/low) × likelihood (high/low). Each quadrant maps to a fixed test-investment pattern: HH = heavy automation + exploratory + monitoring (the team's main investment), HL = automation + targeted exploratory, LH = automation only, LL = monitor-only (no test investment). Areas in HH cannot be moved to LL without explicit risk-acceptance sign-off.
rule.rationale: The quadrant-to-investment map is the load-bearing decision. Without it, the matrix is a heatmap that produces no action. The mapping makes the matrix prescriptive, not descriptive.

example.title: Quadrant investment patterns
example.code:
HH (high impact, high likelihood): billing-subscription-lifecycle → mandatory unit + integration + e2e + monthly exploratory + alerting
HL (high impact, low likelihood): tenant-isolation-and-data-residency → mandatory unit + integration + targeted exploratory + alerting
LH (low impact, high likelihood): search-and-filtering → automation only (unit + light integration), no exploratory
LL (low impact, low likelihood): admin-impersonation-and-audit-log → monitor-only (assertions in logs); no investment until impact or likelihood changes

rule.statement: The matrix is refreshed before every release cycle (sprint, monthly, quarterly — whichever matches the team's cadence). Refresh: recompute likelihood from data, review impact for any area where a new contract / feature / regulatory change applies, regenerate the heatmap and investment plan. A matrix older than the most recent release cycle is stale and blocks new test-strategy commitments.
rule.rationale: Risk landscapes shift quickly: a new feature ships, an incident reveals a previously underestimated area, a new regulation lands. A stale matrix is worse than no matrix because it gives false confidence in obsolete priorities. The refresh ritual keeps the artifact current.

list.item: 5 — Payment processing failure for any tenant; data loss for > 100 users; regulatory breach with named fine
4 — Email outage for 24h; auth lock-out for > 5% of customers; visible UI breakage on the landing page
3 — Search returns degraded results; export is slow; non-critical feature unavailable with documented workaround
2 — Minor UI glitch; small reporting delay; advanced feature degraded
1 — Internal admin convenience; never-customer-visible code path
